intro:
Heaven (Hebrew šāmayim, a plural “the heavens”; Greek ouranos) covers in Scripture a cluster of related but distinct realities that context must distinguish. In its broadest sense “the heavens and the earth” names the totality of creation (Genesis 1:1). More narrowly the heavens are the visible sky—the atmospheric expanse God called Heaven (Genesis 1:8), the home of clouds and birds, sun, moon, and stars. Highest of all is the “heaven of heavens,” the realm of God's throne that transcends the created cosmos: “Behold, heaven and the highest heaven cannot contain you” (1 Kings 8:27; cf. Deuteronomy 10:14). Paul speaks of being “caught up to the third heaven”—to paradise (2 Corinthians 12:2–4), reflecting a Jewish sense of layered heights crowned by the immediate presence of God.

Theologically heaven is above all the dwelling place of God—“Heaven is my throne, and the earth is my footstool” (Isaiah 66:1); hence Jesus teaches us to pray, “Our Father in heaven” (Matthew 6:9). It is the court of the angelic hosts who do his will and behold his face (Matthew 18:10; Hebrews 12:22). Yet heaven is never sealed off from earth: Jacob saw a ladder joining the two with angels ascending and descending (Genesis 28:12), Elijah was taken up in a whirlwind (2 Kings 2:11), and at Jesus' baptism the heavens were torn open (Mark 1:10). The God who is enthroned on high is also the God who “hears in heaven” the prayers of his people (1 Kings 8:30).

The decisive movement comes in Christ. He “came down from heaven” to do the Father's will (John 6:38), and after his resurrection he ascended and was seated at God's right hand (Acts 1:9–11; Hebrews 8:1), going to prepare a place for his own: “In my Father's house are many rooms… I will come again and will take you to myself” (John 14:2–3). The believer's treasure, citizenship, and inheritance are therefore said to be kept in heaven (Matthew 6:20; Philippians 3:20; 1 Peter 1:4), and to depart this life is to be “with Christ” (Philippians 1:23).

Yet the Bible's final hope is not the flight of souls to a distant heaven but the marriage of heaven and earth. The prophets foretold “new heavens and a new earth” (Isaiah 65:17; 2 Peter 3:13), and John sees the holy city, the New Jerusalem, descending out of heaven so that “the dwelling place of God is with man” (Revelation 21:1–4). Heaven, then, is finally not the abolition of the material creation but its renewal under the complete and unmediated presence of God—where his servants “will see his face” (Revelation 22:4). This is the great consolation of the gospel: God will dwell with his people, every tear wiped away, and the redeemed will share an inheritance that is “imperishable, undefiled, and unfading.”
